coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional hybrid nanoconstruct of zerovalent iron and carbon dots for magnetic resonance angiography and optical imaging: An In vivo study
coredata.dc:creator: Nimi, N.
Saraswathy, Ariya
Nazeer, Shaiju S.
Francis, Nimmi
Shenoy, Sachin J.
Jayasree, Ramapurath S.
coredata.dc:description: In magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), gadolinium (Gd) complexes are very often used as contrast agents to enhance the signal from soft tissue deformities and vascular anomalies, to improve the accuracy of diagnosis. The safety concern of using Gd complexes in renally compromised patients pose limitations on its application. To overcome this scenario, we introduce a nontoxic zerovalent iron based nanoparticle as a novel contrast agent for MR angiography and a hybrid version of the same to serve as a dual function contrast agent for targeted liver imaging. The synthesized zerovalent iron (ZVI) nanoparticles after citrate stabilization (C@ZVI) had an average size of 10 nm and exhibited paramagnetic property which is a prerequisite for a positive MRI contrast agent. The longitudinal magnetic relaxivity, r1 of C@ZVI was 4.93 mM−1s−1 which is much higher than that of clinically used Gd based agent, gadoterate meglumine (3.6 mM−1s−1). For multimodal imaging of the liver, initially the ZVI nanoparticle was tailored with a highly liver specific polysaccharide pullulan, and later with fluorescent carbon dots (Cdts) facilitating both optical and MR imaging. The magnetic relaxivity was retained in P@ZVI-Cdts for T1 contrast imaging with an r1 value of 3.48 mM−1s−1. The in vivo MR angiogram using C@ZVI and the liver targeted MRI and optical imaging using P@ZVI-Cdts were successfully demonstrated proving their potential as MRA contrast agent and a liver specific multimodal imaging agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zerovalent iron nanoparticle
Paramagnetic iron nanoparticles
T1 contrast agent
Optical imaging
Magnetic resonance angiogram

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Biomaterials at the Nanoscale for Diagnostic Systems

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of zerovalent iron nanoparticles (ZVI, C@ZVI and P@ZVI)
Synthesis of carbon nanodots coated with pullulan stabilized iron nanoparticles (P@ZVI-Cdts)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support received from Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology under the OHF project. Authors also thank the Scientists-In- Charge and staff of BST, Central Analytical Facility, BCL and DTERT divisions of BMT Wing and HOD, IS &IR for extending their support in using some of their facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Hydrodynamic size distribution of (a) C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts and TEM images showing the particle size of (b) C@ZVI, magnified image of the marked portion is shown as inset showing the core shell structure. Scale bar of inset picture is 20 nm, (c) P@ZVI-Cdts and (d) Cdts.

(a) XRD pattern of P@ZVI-Cdts, C@ZVI and Cdts (b) FTIR spectra of C@ZVI, Cdts andP@ZVI-Cdts (c) Raman spectra of Cdts and P@ZVI-Cdts showing the characteristic D and G bands. Inset shows the magnified version to see the G band.

(a) M-H behavior of C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts, T1 weighted MR phantom images of (b) C@ZVI, (c) P@ZVI-Cdts for varying concentrations (0.017–0.179) mM and r1 and r2 relaxation rates of (d) C@ZVI, (e) P@ZI-Cdts with the r1 values 4.93 and 3.34 mMS−1respectively.
(a)UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Cdts and P@ZVI-Cdts showing an absorbance around 320 nm, Fluorescence emission spectra of (b) Cdts and (c) P@ZVI-Cdts under different excitations.

C@ZVI enhanced Magnetic Resonance Angiography (MRA) of rat model (a) Control animal (b) C@ZVI administrated animal (c) Signal intensity difference calculated from different organs of live animal images of pre and post MRA.
The T1 weighted MR imaging of P@ZVI-Cdts (a) Control animal (b) P@ZVI-Cdts administrated animal (c) and (d) Color coded images of (a) and (b) for better visualization.

In vivo fluorescence images of P@ZVI-Cdts injected mice at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm and emission wavelength of 640 nm. (a) Live animal in vivo imaging (b) Bio-distribution of the materials as observed from the fluorescence signal from excised organs.
Representing the interactions involved in the pullulan stabilized carbon coated zerovalent iron nanoparticle.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional hybrid nanoconstruct of zerovalent iron and carbon dots for magnetic resonance angiography and optical imaging: An In vivo study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Biophotonics and Imaging, Biomedical Technology Wing, Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology, Poojappura, Thiruvananthapuram, 695 012 Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of In vivo Models and Testing, Biomedical Technology Wing, Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology, Poojappura, Thiruvananthapuram, 695 012 Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of In vivo Models and Testing
Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: Department of Physics, NSS College, Pandalam, Kerala, India.
Present address: Department of Pathology, University of Illinois at Chicago (UIC), Chicago, Illinois, USA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), gadolinium (Gd) complexes are very often used as contrast agents to enhance the signal from soft tissue deformities and vascular anomalies, to improve the accuracy of diagnosis. The safety concern of using Gd complexes in renally compromised patients pose limitations on its application. To overcome this scenario, we introduce a nontoxic zerovalent iron based nanoparticle as a novel contrast agent for MR angiography and a hybrid version of the same to serve as a dual function contrast agent for targeted liver imaging. The synthesized zerovalent iron (ZVI) nanoparticles after citrate stabilization (C@ZVI) had an average size of 10 nm and exhibited paramagnetic property which is a prerequisite for a positive MRI contrast agent. The longitudinal magnetic relaxivity, r1 of C@ZVI was 4.93 mM−1s−1 which is much higher than that of clinically used Gd based agent, gadoterate meglumine (3.6 mM−1s−1). For multimodal imaging of the liver, initially the ZVI nanoparticle was tailored with a highly liver specific polysaccharide pullulan, and later with fluorescent carbon dots (Cdts) facilitating both optical and MR imaging. The magnetic relaxivity was retained in P@ZVI-Cdts for T1 contrast imaging with an r1 value of 3.48 mM−1s−1. The in vivo MR angiogram using C@ZVI and the liver targeted MRI and optical imaging using P@ZVI-Cdts were successfully demonstrated proving their potential as MRA contrast agent and a liver specific multimodal imaging agent.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles have been extensively studied for their unique and size-dependent electronic, optical and magnetic properties and their applications in biomedical imaging [1]. Recently, various imaging probes have been designed by combining two or more functional nanoparticles for multimodal imaging facilitating efficient and accurate diagnosis [2]. Among the imaging techniques, MRI is the most powerful one with exceptional soft tissue contrast to acquire both anatomical and functional information when compared to other imaging modalities like computed tomography [3]. During imaging, it is very common to introduce chemical compounds called contrast agents as supplements to maximize the ability of imaging tools to improve the visibility of the image. Currently offered MRI contrast agents are mostly based on gadolinium and its complexes, and are being adorned as the most valued ones for medical diagnosis and is also considered safe. However, some of them cause severe long term toxicity like nephrogenic systemic fibrosis to patients suffering from kidney failure. Moreover, it is reported that in some cases there are chances of gadolinium deposition in the brain [4]. Considering this background, we have tried to develop an alternate candidate for the existing gadolinium based contrast agents. Here, we report a modified zerovalent iron nanoparticle as a novel MRI contrast agent and its efficacy to use as an excellent agent for magnetic resonance angiography. Modifying zerovalent iron with liver targeting polysaccharide, pullulan and fluorescent carbon dots, it gains additional properties to use as a new multimodal imaging probe for both MRI and optical imaging, specifically for liver diseases. A typical MRI contrast agent is expected to shorten the transverse, T2/T2* (negative contrast), or the longitudinal, T1 (positive contrast) relaxation times of protons in the water molecules. Iron-based nanoparticles, especially ultra small superparamagnetic iron oxide (USPIO), superparamagnetic iron oxide (SPION) and manganese substituted SPION have attracted researchers for its capability to use as MRI contrast agents [5–8]. Very small iron nanoparticles are either in the superparamagnetic or paramagnetic regime and their stable dispersions with the high magnetic moment favors their use in MR imaging [9,10]. Due to the super paramagnetic nature of iron oxide, it generates T2 contrast or negative contrast where a decrease in the pixel intensity is observed [11]. Whereas, T1 contrast agents give signal enhanced positive imaging with high spatial resolution, and they generally don't disrupt the homogeneity over a large dimension. Hence, T1 contrast agents are preferred over T2 contrast agents in MRI [12,13]. Moreover, T2 pulse sequences are not suitable for the diagnosis of many pathologic conditions where calcium or other metal deposits and bleeding are expected, as they themselves will generate endogenous negative contrast. This becomes more complicated in cardiovascular imaging and magnetic resonance angiography. Hence, as of today, the toxic gadolinium-based contrast agents remain as the sole option for clinical applications. Therefore the need to develop a nontoxic, biocompatible T1 contrast agent for magnetic resonance imaging emerges and hence forms the basis of this study.

Zerovalent Iron (ZVI) nanoparticle is known for their excellent environmental remediation due to its high reactivity, larger surface area, cost effectiveness and eco friendliness. The reducing property of ZVI nanoparticles are exploited for environmental remediation, quick separation of contaminants in waste water treatment [14,15] and in sensing applications [16,17]. ZVI nanoparticles have a core-shell structure with a zerovalent iron core surrounded by an iron oxide/hydroxide shell [18–20]. They are highly reactive and have a tendency to oxidize to various forms of iron oxides. Due to this reactivity, ZVI nanoparticles have to be suitably surface modified to avoid toxicity and to tailor to the needs of the application. Since the magnetic property of ZVI resembles that of gadolinium, it is expected to generate T1 or positive contrast for the images. Hence in this work, we have made an attempt to use ZVI nanoparticles to use as a positive contrast agent for MRI. The main advantage of using modified ZVI nanoparticles as MRI contrast agent compared with gadolinium-based contrast agents is their non-toxicity. Moreover, as in the case of iron oxide nanoparticles, the iron nanoparticles are expected to clear from the circulation primarily by the reticuloendothelial system [21].

We have synthesized citrate coated magnetic ZVI nanoparticles (C@ZVI) for magnetic resonance angiography and by exploiting the complementary properties of the materials, the same is modified for targeted optical and MR imaging of the liver. Non-toxicity and the magnetic property of C@ZVI were favorable to use it as contrast agent for magnetic resonance angiography (MRA). Later, a multimodal contrast agent for liver imaging was prepared by designing a hybrid nanoconstruct comprising ZVI and fluorescent carbon dots (Cdts) along with liver targeting polysaccharide, pullulan. The excellent physico-chemical and biological properties and the non toxicity compared to semiconductor quantum dots are the rationale for choosing Cdts with size less than 10 nm [22,23]. Cdts are also known for its photostability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, outstanding water solubility, high sensitivity and excellent selectivity to target analytes in addition to bio-imaging and bio-sensing potential [24,25]. Added advantages are tunable fluorescence emission and excitation ranging from UV to NIR region [26]. Moreover, Cdts have high quantum yield (QY) and large Stokes shift which enable them as a promising candidate for fluorescence imaging, an alternative to the toxic cadmium-selenium quantum dots [27].

The novelty of the present work relies on the development of ZVI as a non-toxic, biocompatible and efficient positive contrast agent for MRA and added to it is the synthesis of P@ZVI-Cdts which has specific application in liver targeted multifunctional optical and MR imaging. A systematic characterization and in vitro and in vivo imaging of both systems have been carried out and reported here.

Iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4.7H2O), sodium borohydride, trisodium citrate, diphosphorous pentoxide (P2O5) and glacial acetic acid were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and pullulan was purchased from Fluka, Sigma – Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dialysis membrane (3500Da), Quinine sulfate dehydrate, KBr, Xylazine hydrochloride, Ketamine hydrochloride and Atropine were purchased from Sigma – Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), MEM medium and Fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Thermofisher scientific (Waltham, MA, United States) and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was from HiMedia Laboratories (LLC, Pennsylvania, USA). All other chemicals and reagents used were of analytical reagent grade (Sigma- Aldrich).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of zerovalent iron nanoparticles (ZVI, C@ZVI and P@ZVI)
ce:section.ce:para:
The zerovalent iron nanoparticles were prepared by the chemical reduction of ferrous sulfate using sodium borohydride [28]. Briefly, 20 mL of 10 mM ferrous sulfate solution was prepared and 5 mL of 25 mM sodium borohydride was added to it. The black precipitate resulted was isolated by magnetic separation and washed with ethanol. In the absence of a protective coating, ZVI nanoparticles will undergo oxidative degradation at their surface. In addition, the particle uptake by the cells and tissue will be limited without surface functionalization. To avoid surface oxidation and aggregation, and also to improve the biological property, trisodium citrate is used as a stabilizing agent. For this, the as prepared ZVI was stirred overnight with 10 mL of 100 mM trisodium citrate. The excess of the regents was removed by precipitating it with an excess of acetone. Formation of a layer of citrate ions over ZVI nanoparticle prevents oxidation.
4 F
e
2 +
+
B
H 4
−
+ 3
H 2
O → 4 F
e 0
+
H 3
B
O 3
+ 7
H +

The formation of C@ZVI was confirmed and the physico-chemical and biological characterizations were performed before using the materials for in vivo Magnetic Resonance Angiography. Later, the nanoparticles were coated with water soluble polysaccharide pullulan, by stirring overnight with 0.35 mg/mL of pullulan (MW-100000) at 1100 RPM to get P@ZVI.

Carbon nanodots were prepared by carbonization reaction using acetic acid as carbon precursor [29]. Briefly, 2.5 g of phosphorous pentoxide was weighed into a beaker and 900 μl of glacial acetic acid along with 100 μl of water was added spontaneously into it. The brown colored slurry obtained after 5min was washed by centrifugation and finally dialyzed with MWCO 3500Da dialysis membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon nanodots coated with pullulan stabilized iron nanoparticles (P@ZVI-Cdts)
ce:section.ce:para: 10 mL of P@ZVI, having a concentration of 10 mg/mL, was stirred well with 500 μl of carbon nanodots. The reaction was allowed to continue for 2 h for efficient coating of carbon dots on the surface of P@ZVI. Then it was washed by centrifugation. C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts were characterized for their size, surface charge, magnetic and biological properties.

The characterizations of the samples at every stage were performed for its physico-chemical properties like size, functionalization, magnetic properties, optical properties etc. and also for biological properties like cytocompatibility, blood compatibility and in vivo imaging properties.

The particle size and zeta potential of the as-synthesized nanoparticles C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts were analyzed by photon correlation spectroscopy and laser doppler anemometry, respectively, with a Zeta Sizer, Nano ZS with MPT-2 auto titrator (Malvern Instruments Limited, UK) at 25 °C.

The morphology of C@ZVI, Cdts and P@ZVI-Cdts nanoparticles were examined by transmission electron microscopy at 100 kV (TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). For this, dilute solution of the samples was drop cast on to a formvar-coated copper grid.

The synthesized nanoparticles C@ZVI, Cdts and P@ZVI-Cdts were studied for its surface stabilization and structure using a FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrometer (USA)) in the diffuse reflectance (DRIFT) mode. A total of 2% (w/w) of the sample was mixed with dry potassium bromide (KBr). The mixture was ground to a fine powder using a mortar, and the powder was scanned at 4 mm/s at a resolution of 4 cm−1 between 500 and 4000 cm−1. The Raman spectra of bare Cdts and P@ZVI-Cdts were also recorded using a confocal Raman microscope (Witec alpha 300RA Raman spectrometer) using a laser source of 532 nm.

The method of X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to investigate the structure of C@ZVI nanoparticles, Cdts and P@ZVI-Cdts. The phase analysis of the nanoparticles was carried out using X'Pert PRO X-ray diffraction (XRD) instrumentation. The crystal structure was determined by analyzing the position and intensity of diffraction peaks observed in the diffraction angle range, 20° - 80° using Cu Kα radiation of 1.5406A° at 40 kV and a 20 mA current.

The Magnetic properties of the powder samples of C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts nanoparticles were measured at room temperature using a SQUID-Vibration Sample Magnetometer (SQUID-VSM, Quantum Design). This important experiment gives an understanding about the magnetic property of the nano materials.

The magnetic relaxivity was measured using 1.5T whole body MR scanner (MAGNETOM Avanto Tim System 1.5T - Siemens, Munich, Germany) equipped with a 12-channel head coil. Relaxivity is the measure of the efficiency of the contrast material to enhance the proton relaxation rates. In vitro spin-lattice relaxation r1 using T1 weighted images and spin-spin relaxation r2 using T2 weighted images were acquired using phantoms of the materials prepared in deionized water, with concentrations of iron ranging between 0.017 and 0.179 mM. Images were acquired from different planes of the samples with a separation of 6 mm at a temperature of 22 °C with an FOV equivalent to 255 × 340 mm.

An inversion recovery sequence was used for the measurement of the longitudinal (T1) relaxation time. The repetition time (TR) and echo time (TE) was set at 8250 and 10 ms, respectively, and the MR signal was measured by changing the inversion time (TI), as 50, 100, 300, 700, 1200, 2000, 3000 and 4000 ms. A modified Transverse (T2) relaxometry spin echo sequence was run at three different planes of the phantoms for T2 relaxometry measurements. For this, TR was set at 2000 ms and the MR signal was measured by changing the TE values from 15 to 120 ms. From the resulting MRI maps, pixel intensity with respect to each concentration was extracted. Signal intensity against TE values was plotted and using curve fitting method, the T1 and T2 relaxation time was calculated. This procedure was repeated for ten iron concentrations to obtain both T1 and T2 relaxation times. Longitudinal and transverse relaxation values (r1 and r2) were calculated and T1 and T2 relaxation rates were plotted against the iron concentration and relaxivity value was determined by the linear fit [30].

The UV–Vis absorption of the hybrid construct was carried out using Shimadzu UV Spectrophotometer UV-1800 and the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of Cdts and P@ZVI-Cdts were measured at different excitation wavelengths in the range 330–600 nm using the SPECTRACQ fluorescence spectrophotometer. The quantum yield for the Cdts was also determined using quinine sulfate as a reference, as its absorption and emission matches with that of the Cdts, with an absorbance at 320 nm and the emission covering a broad range from 360 nm to 700 nm. The fluorescence spectra of Cdts and quinine sulfate were recorded at an excitation of 320 nm. By comparing the integrated PL intensities (excited at 320 nm) and the absorbance values of the Cdts with the reference quinine sulfate, quantum yield of the Cdts sample was determined [31].

The samples were checked for their blood compatibility by examining the hemolysis and the blood cell aggregation response. For hemolysis assay, human blood collected from volunteers was centrifuged at 700RPM for 10min. The RBC thus obtained was washed and diluted with saline. Then 100 μl of C@ZVI nanoparticles and P@ZVI-Cdts at different concentrations (50,100, 200 mM) were dispersed in saline and was added to 100 μl of washed RBC and mixed gently and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. The supernatant was collected and read at 541 nm by UV spectrophotometer (Carywin UV). Saline was used as the negative control and distilled water as a positive control. The RBC, WBC, and platelet aggregation study were carried out to rule out the aggregation of the blood cells when in contact with a foreign body for intravenous injection when used as a contrast agent, as well as for optical imaging in vivo. For this, 200 μg of the C@ZVI nanoparticles and P@ZVI-Cdts, were added to 100 μl of the diluted blood cells and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. Here saline was used as the negative control. Aggregation, if any, was detected through phase contrast microscope (Leica DM IRB, Germany) at 20× magnification [32].

To test the cellular response, in vitro cytotoxicity of the nanoparticles was evaluated on mouse fibroblast cell lines (L929) using MTT assay as per ISO 10993–5:2009 standard. The cells were cultured in MEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and kept at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2. Cells were seeded in 24 well plates at a density of 0.5 × 105 cells per well and incubated for 24 h with 25, 50, 100 and 200 mM concentrations of nanoparticles. The medium containing test material was removed and MTT was added to each well at a concentration of 100 μg/well and the well plates were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. The absorbance was measured using a plate reader (Finstruments Microplate Reader USA). Percentage viability was calculated from the ratio of absorbance of the test sample to the absorbance of negative control. The cells cultured in MEM medium with 10% FBS was used as negative control.

Wistar rats weighing 200–250 g were used for MR angiographic studies using C@ZVI nanoparticles. The institutional ethics committee approved the entire animal experiments (No.SCT/ABS/IAEC-83/11 dated 21.02.2014). MRA was performed by using a 1.5T MRI scanner (Magnatom Avanto; Siemens, Munich, Germany) equipped with a 12-channel head and neck coil. Animals were anesthetized with ketamine (100 mg/kg), xylazine (7 mg/kg) and atropine (0.02 mg/kg) and imaged before and after intravenous tail vein administration of the C@ZVI nanoparticles. After running the routine MRI sequences for acquiring the localizer and T1 weighted sequence, MR angiogram was performed using a 3D-FLASH dynamic contrast-enhanced MRA sequence. Imaging parameters for 3D-FLASH were a TR of 9 ms, a TE of 3.27 ms, a 30° flip angle, a 256 × 232 matrix 250 mm FOV, and a single slab with 17 sections of 1.00-mm effective thickness and 20% interslice gap. The MR angiography was obtained immediately after the bolus intravenous administration of (2.83 mg Fe/kg) iron nanoparticles followed by a saline flush.

The animals were anesthetized with ketamine (100 mg/kg), xylazine (7 mg/kg) and atropine (0.02 mg/kg) before MR imaging. For the MR imaging of the liver, a coronal T1 weighted spin-echo sequence was used for localization. A spin echo (SE) T1 weighted sequence having the parameters, TR = 582 ms, TE = 11 ms, matrix = 266 × 512 and slice thickness 3 mm was used. The field of view was 87 × 140 mm to include the whole animal in their entirety. The acquisition time was 1 min and 32 s for the T1 weighted sequence. The pre contrast images were acquired without the administration of nanoparticles, after which a dose of 2.97 mg Fe/kg, P@ZVI-Cdts was injected intravenously through the tail vein. SE image acquisitions were done within 10–15 min to get axial and coronal T1 weighted images.

Male Swiss albino mice with an average body weight of 35 g were used for fluorescence bioimaging. The animals were supplied from the institute animal facility and the studies were approved by the institute's animal ethics committee (No. SCT/ABS/IAEC-82/6 dated 11.11.2013). The mice were anesthetized using ketamine (70 mg/kg) and xylazine (5 mg/kg) before nanoparticle injection and imaging. 200 μl of the P@ZVI-Cdts nanoparticle (435 μg Fe/kg) in aqueous solution was injected intravenously and the animals were imaged immediately and after 2 h, using Xenogen IVIS Spectrum imaging system (Caliper Life Sciences, Hopkinton, MA, USA). Various excitation wavelengths, including the NIR emission of P@ZVI-Cdts were tested for the in vivo imaging of the mice. The liver targeting capability, as well as the biodistribution, along with the clearance of the nanoparticles coated with pullulan was studied. Later, the organs like brain, heart, lungs, liver, kidneys and spleen were harvested after 4 h of intravenous injection and were imaged ex vivo for the bio distribution assay.

The study evaluates the potential of suitably modified zerovalent iron to use as imaging agent. Accordingly, novel zerovalent iron based positive contrast agents, C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts were synthesized and tested for MR Angiography and liver-specific bimodal imaging. Initially, C@ZVI nanoparticles were synthesized and tested for MR angiography and in the later stage, C@ZVI was modified by replacing citrate with pullulan, to introduce liver specificity, and also was coated with fluorescent carbon dots to use it as a multimodal nanoconstruct for MRI as well as fluorescence imaging of liver related disorders. The initial material, ZVI nanoparticles were prepared by a simple and cost-effective technique of chemical reduction of Fe2+ ions to Fe0 using sodium borohydride. The recovered ZVI nanoparticles were washed with ethanol to prevent the rapid oxidation of the nanoparticles. Though the uncoated ZVI has been extensively used in areas like environmental remediation and waste water management, its biomedical application in the bare form is not advisable due to its characteristic electron donor property which could lead to the production of hydroxyl ions in the system. To avoid this, trisodium citrate was coated onto ZVI nanoparticles, wherein the organic acids like citric acid can act as a ligand which can form complexes with iron oxide present in the shell of ZVI. The carboxylate group of citrate binds with that of Fe atoms and form complexes on the surface of iron oxide thereby blocking the reactive sites, preventing it from further oxidation. Thus, trisodium citrate functions as a stabilizing agent, by the formation of a layer of citrate ions on the ZVI nanoparticles preventing it from more oxidation and aggregation [33,34]. Surface modification of nanoparticle plays a vital role in biomedical applications, which imparts added weightage to biodistribution, reducing toxicity and clearance from the body when used in vivo. A thin layer of surface coating will help the nanoparticles to retain their original properties, which otherwise may lose due to factors like aggregation, self assembly etc. Here, the stabilizing agent, sodium citrate also helps the nanoparticles to achieve good water dispersibility which might be attributed to the reduced surface energy and dipolar attraction of the magnetic nanoparticles after modification with sodium citrate [35].

Later, the ZVI nanoparticle was surface modified with a polysaccharide polymer to introduce liver targeting property for the developed system P@ZVI. Here physical adsorption of pullulan had taken place to substitute the unstable nanoparticle suspension. Later, carbon dots were prepared by the rapid mixing of acetic acid with P2O5. Here, the acetic acid acts as a carbon precursor as well as a polar solvent and P2O5 serves as a strong dehydrating agent which helps in generating defective carbon. The carbonization can be termed as a bottom-up and wet-chemical method [29]. The heat generated due to the reaction between acetic acid and P2O5 is responsible for the production of Cdts. Then, the as prepared carbon dots were coated onto P@ZVI nanoparticles where hydrogen bonding is expected to occur between the carboxylic/hydroxyl groups of the carbon dots with the hydroxyl groups present in P@ZVI to get P@ZVI-Cdts. Hence, the probable interaction of the carbon dots with the pullulan modified ZVI would be through hydrogen bonding resulting in a stable carbon coated pullulan stabilized ZVI (P@ZVI-Cdts) nanoparticles. A pictorial representation of the interactions involved in pullulan stabilized zerovalent iron nanoparticles coated with carbon dots is shown in Scheme 1 .

In the case of nanoparticles, a clear understanding about some of the factors like size, morphology, effectiveness of surface functionalization, zeta potential, etc. is required to use it for biological imaging application. The surface functionalization is very relevant because, without efficient protective covering bare ZVI readily causes aggregation and undergoes oxidation. The hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique showed particles with spherical morphology with a size of 10 nm and 12 nm respectively (Fig. 1 a). The results were further confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1b and c). The DLS data reveal a narrow distribution with a single peak corresponding to the narrow size range of the particles, with least aggregation. Zeta potential of C@ZVI suspended in normal saline with 0.9% NaCl, at neutral pH was -9 mV and this material was found to be stable for more than 6 months, without showing any aggregation. The negative value observed is due to the presence of citrate covering on the surface of ZVI. The presence of citrate coating helps the nanoparticles to stabilize the suspension by electrostatic repulsion [36]. On coating with Cdts, the zeta potential of P@ZVI-Cdts has slightly increased to −15 mV. This increase is attributed to the carboxylic groups present in the carbon dots, incorporated in the final construct. The final system is also found to be stable for more than six months. The size and stability of the polymer modified nanoparticles are highly dependent on the molecular weight of the polymers used. The higher the molecular weight, more stable the nanoparticles will be [37]. Here the polymer used was of fairly high molecular weight which ensures least aggregation of the particles due to the decrease in inter-particle dipole-dipole forces caused by the steric hindrance induced by the polymer coating [38]. Even with a zeta potential of −15 mV, the suspension was found to be stable by counteracting particle attractive forces which results in limited aggregation. Hence from the obtained zeta potential it is evident that the stabilization is by the steric hindrance and not by ionic repulsion [39–41]. TEM based quantitative size analysis showed a clear core-shell structure for C@ZVI where Fe0 forms the core whereas the oxides and hydroxides are responsible for the shell and the citrate forms a coating over the nanoparticles. In the synthesis of C@ZVI, the ZVI nanoparticles were washed with ethanol prior to the citrate coating, which prevents it from rapid oxidation. But a partial oxidation is expected to take place before the complex formation with citrate molecules, resulting in a very thin oxide shell. The thickness of the shell seemed to differ considerably depending on the extent of oxidation. The shell thickness may vary from around 2 nm to nearly 25 nm according to oxidation caused by aging [15]. Here the thickness of the shell is found to be around 2–3 nm whereas the core has a diameter of less than 8 nm (Fig. 1b). Hence, we presume that only a partial oxidation has taken place over ZVI when compared to highly oxidised shell with thickness nearly 25 nm [18]. The partial oxidation followed by the surface modification of zerovalent iron is expected to reduce the toxicity to a great extent [42]. The carbon dots synthesized through the carbonization process and the carbon dots coated pullulan stabilized particles showed an average size of 5 nm and 12 nm respectively (Fig. 1d and c). A core-shell structure was absent in the final system (Fig. 1c). This may be due to the formation of an infinitesimal shell that was not evident under TEM imaging or might be due to the hindrance/contrast difference of the carbon dots which would have masked the shell structure on TEM images. No aggregation was observed in the TEM images which indicate the formation of stable hybrid nanoconstructs of ZVI and Cdts.

The FTIR spectrum of core-shell C@ZVI showed a sharp peak at 1134 cm−1 corresponding to the CO stretching. The peaks at 1392 and 1595 cm−1 are assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of the carboxyl groups of the capping agent, sodium citrate. The broader band at 3429 cm−1 confirms the presence of OH groups suggesting the formation of ferrioxyhydroxide (FeOOH) layer on zerovalent iron nanoparticles [43]. Thus, from the data obtained we could arrive at a conclusion that coating of citrate, as well as a layer of oxyhydroxides, had taken place over the zerovalent iron nanoparticles. The presence of pullulan and Cdts coated on the surface of ZVI was also investigated using FTIR, wherein the presence of OH groups of pullulan and Cdts were confirmed by the presence of broader band at 3400 cm−1 and 3100 cm−1 respectively. The peak at 1680 cm−1 corresponds to that of the CC- vibrations of Cdts. The presence of CC- was also confirmed in P@ZVI-Cdts with a slight shift at 1650 cm−1 (Fig. 2 b). Furthermore, the peaks at 1550 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 attributes to the presence of carboxylic groups present in the carbon dots. These peaks were also present in the final construct with slight shift confirming the successful conjugation of carbon dots in P@ZVI-Cdts. The prepared carbon dots were easily dispersible in water proving the presence of carboxylic acid functional group which can contribute to the modification of pullulan coated ZVI nanoparticles by hydrogen bonding [44]. The formation of carbon dots was confirmed by the characteristic D and G bands at 1326 and 1577 cm−1 respectively in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 2c) [45]. Herein the D band is associated with the presence of sp 3 defects and the G band corresponds to the in-plane stretching vibration of sp 2 carbon atoms [46,47]. ID/IG ratio of Cdts was found to be 1.2 indicating the presence of graphitic carbon in the sample. Comparatively high value of ID/IG ratio shows the presence of amorphous carbon in the sample which is in accordance with the XRD data [48,49]. In the hybrid construct P@ZVI-Cdts, the G band appears around 1581 cm−1 with a slight red shift which may be due to the strain effect. The disappearance of D band is attributed to the interaction of alicyclic groups of pullulan forming disorder in the carbon lattice [50]. The peak at 922 cm−1 corresponds to the COC stretching and the peak at 240 cm−1 shows aliphatic chain which again confirmed the presence of pullulan in the hybrid construct.

The crystallinity and the structure of C@ZVI, Cdts and P@ZVI-Cdts were determined by the XRD technique (Fig. 2a). The XRD pattern showed a sharp peak at 45.5° confirming the crystalline nature of C@ZVI. The peak at 45.5° corresponds to the body-centered cubic lattices with (110) plane [51]. The peaks at 31.7°(220) and 37°(311) showed the presence of oxides of iron, magnetite [30]. This oxide formation agrees well with the TEM observation where clear core-shell morphology was observed. Thus, this result indicates that the synthesized particles are Fe0 with oxide contamination. A broad peak at 26° in the XRD analysis of Cdts suggests that the synthesized carbondots are non-crystalline nature. The 26° peak representing the (002) plane of indicating that the Cdts has a graphite structure [26,52]. Peaks corresponding to ZVI and Cdts were seen in the final construct P@ZVI-Cdts with a slight shift in peak at around 26.2° and 46.2°. The coating of pullulan along with carbon dots might have contributed to the shift in the peaks. The peaks acorresponding to the oxides at 37° was seen with a very low intensity confirming only an infinetisiminal oxide shell is formed in the final construct which is agreeing with the TEM observation.

The field dependent magnetization of the samples measured at room temperature (Fig. 3 a) showed that the zerovalent iron core-shell nanoparticles exhibited paramagnetic behavior. The hybrid consruct, P@ZVI-Cdts also showed paramagnetic property, with reduced magnetization strength. The paramagnetic behavior of the nanoparticles is attributed to the unpaired electrons of the iron core and the magnetization of C@ZVI, which is in agreement with previous reports on nanoparticles comprising core-shell iron of similar size range [53,54]. For smaller nanoparticles, the magnetization at room temperature will tend to decrease. The spin canting effect, which marks the lack of full alignment of the spins in the surface atoms, may also be responsible for the low magnetization of the nanoparticles [55].

To scrutinize the possibility of using C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts as T1 MRI contrast agent, the magnetic relaxation time of both materials were measured on a 1.5T applied magnetic field. Fig. 3b and c represents the T1 weighted images of the phantoms with different concentrations of Fe in both C@ZVI and the hybrid construct. The T1weighed MR images of the phantoms showed better image contrast as assessed by the increased pixel intensity, with the increase in Fe concentrations. The longitudinal relaxivity value, r1 of C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts were 4.93 mM−1s−1 and 3.34 mM−1s−1respectively. The effect of surface coating with pullulan and Cdts has slightly reduced the magnetic property and hence the r1 value of P@ZVI-Cdts. However, compared to the r1 value of commercially available and widely used gadolinium-based contrast agent Dotarem (3.6 mM−1s−1), C@ZVI has much better value for r1. The transverse relaxivity (r2) was estimated as 29.76 mM−1s−1 and 19.16 mM−1s−1for C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts respectively, which are very low when compared to the iron oxide systems used as T2 contrast agents. The r2/r1 ratio is an important parameter to assess the competence of the materials as T1 contrast agents. Here, the r2/r1 was found to be 6.03 and 5.73 respectively, for C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts signifying its potential as a T1 contrast agent (Fig. 3d and e). The high longitudinal relaxivity of the nanoparticles, compared to that of the commercially available ones, can be attributed to the relatively small size of the core and the weak magnetization exhibited by the nanoparticles due to the increase in the surface disorder caused by surface spin canting effects. The pronounced longitudinal relaxivity of iron nanoparticles, when compared to other T1 contrast agents, suggests their potential to use it as a positive contrast agent.

The optical property of Cdts and P@ZVI-Cdts was evaluated using UV–Vis and fluorescence emission spectroscopic techniques. The absorption maximum of Cdts in aqueous medium was at 285 nm with a shoulder at around 320 nm. These two absorption peaks shown in UV–Vis curve (Fig. 4 a), were assigned to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively of the CC and CN bonds, signifying the existence of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The hybrid construct, P@ZVI-Cdts showed a maximum absorption at 345 nm (Fig. 4a). Cdts as well as P@ZVI-Cdts showed excitation dependent fluorescence behavior [56], with a broad emission ranging from 500 to 650 nm and excitation ranging from 390 to 540 nm (Fig. 4b and c). As the excitation wavelength is increased from 390 to 540 nm, emission intensity decreases along with a red shift in the emission maximum from 500 to 650 nm. Quantum efficiency evaluated against quinine sulfate gave a very good yield of 23% for Cdts. To check the potential of the Cdts for imaging applications, the fluorescence imaging efficacy of P@ZVI-Cdts was tested for different concentrations (50, 100, 200, 250 and 300 mM) using an optical imaging system (Fig. 4 in Ref. [61]). Concentration dependent variation in the photoluminescence was observed at an excitation wavelength of 540 nm with the emission at 640 nm. Lower energy wavelengths were chosen to suit the imaging applications to avoid interference from autofluorescence while considering in vivo imaging. Usually, fluorescent carbon dots exhibit such a broad emission and excitation-dependent emission phenomenon which occurs possibly due to various energy levels associated with different surface states formed by a range of self passivated functional groups [24,29]. The absorption observed around 330 nm in Cdts can be due to the n-π* transition of CO bonds. The surface state of Cdts was considered to preside over their emission, but the emission was apparently related to the size distribution of Cdts in terms of quantum confinement [57].

The hemolytic response and cytocompatibility of C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts were tested as a prerequisite for in vivo imaging. The percentage hemolysis was determined quantitatively using different concentrations of materials (Fig. 1 in Ref. [61]). The results were encouraging with less than 1% lysis only observed when C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts were in contact with the erythrocytes, compared to the controls used. The results of the aggregation studies also showed no aggregation with RBC, WBC, and platelets (Fig. 2 in Ref. [61]) and the blood cells retained the morphology as in the case of saline. The results of blood compatibility study are favorable to recommend the developed contrast agent for intravenous injection, without causing aggregation with any of the blood cells. To check the cytocompatibility, three samples along with positive and negative controls were taken in triplicate and tested in normal fibroblast cells, L929. The percentage viability of C@ZVI was found to be above 90% for all the concentrations of iron and the viability of P@ZVI-Cdts was around 80% for all the concentrations considered (Fig. 3 in Ref. [61]).

After proving biocompatibility and non-cytotoxicity of the ZVI based systems, we tested the potential of C@ZVI as an MRA contrast agent. MRA is a non-invasive and safe method for the diagnosis of various blood vessels, such as myocardial infarction, atherosclerotic plaque, and tumor angiogenesis without the reported risks of conventional angiography. We performed MRA on rats by intravenous bolus injection of C@ZVI at a dose of 2.83 mg Fe/kg of body weight through the tail vein. Angiography performed immediately after injection showed strong contrast in the vascular system (Fig. 5 a and b). Prominent vascular structures like inferior vena cava which was observed much distended probably due to the bolus infusion, right auricle and ventricles of the heart, the renal veins, superior vena cava and jugular veins were clearly visible in the dorsal view. Pulmonary trunk, pulmonary artery branches and hepatic portal vein were visible in the lateral oblique view (Fig. 6 in Ref. [61]). The signal enhancement in these vascular structures and organs can identify any abnormality, if present facilitating easy diagnosis. The contrast of the liver, kidney and blood vessels of the mesentery were also observed to be enhanced post injection in comparison to pre-contrast. For a better visualization and clear identification of the blood vessels by any non-radiologists, a color coded angiographic image of the animals is shown in Fig. 5 in Ref. [61]. The high relaxivity of the material helped in obtaining a good contrast difference of the circulating system in the in vivo images, which was maintained for 10min of administration, before getting cleared from the system. The pixel intensity measured from the organs revealed marked difference in the intensity of pre and post contrast images (Fig. 5c). The contrast difference was found to be maximum in the heart when compared to the pre-contrast image. The greater veins and the kidney showed 40% and 52% increase in signal intensity, respectively compared with the pre-contrast image. Since the nanoparticles got cleared from the circulatory system quickly, the imaging window was very short. Hence the particles need to be modified further to use as a blood pool agent. It is a known factor that the timings of contrast injection and image acquisition is crucial to achieve high quality MRA without venous involvement [58]. The results could be improved by using an automated contrast injection system and by optimizing the timings. We hope that modified MR sequences with more optimized acquisition parameters would improve the results of the current study. At the same time, the quick clearance of the particles through the kidneys, as observed by the decrease in signal intensity in the MRA after 10min, is advantageous as it could reduce the probable side effects, if any. From the above observations, we could arrive at a conclusion that C@ZVI nanoparticles with high relaxivity and enhanced contrast signal, could serve as a good positive contrast agent for MR angiography with least toxicity and quick renal clearance.

The ZVI nanoparticles prepared was stabilized with pullulan and later coated with cabon dots to obtain the final nanoconstruct P@ZVI-Cdts that could be used for multimodal imaging. To see the liver targeting and MR imaging efficiency of P@ZVI-Cdts, T1 weighted in vivo MR imaging of the animals was carried out within 15 min of intravenous injection of the same. Pre and post injection images showed a marked enhancement in the signal of the liver (Fig. 6 a and b), which is characteristic of a positive MRI contrast agent. For targeted MRI, the contrast agent is supposed to have the specificity towards the organ of interest and should possess good tissue penetration as well. This is to ensure maximum contrast enhancement of the desired site using the lowest possible concentration of the contrast material. The P@ZVI-Cdts nanoconstruct was designed with high liver affinity by introducing liver specific polysaccharide, pullulan as a covering agent over ZVI. The MR images show that the signal enhancement was not prominent in other organs, which confirms the liver targeting potential of P@ZVI-Cdts. The targeting ability is ascribed to the consequence of asialoglycoprotein receptor (ASGPR) mediated liver affinity of pullulan. ASGPR is a C-type lectin, primarily expressed in a polar manner on the sinusoidal and basolateral surface of the plasma hepatocyte membrane. It shows evidence of high affinity for carbohydrates especially galactose, N-acetylgalactosamine and glucose carbohydrate polymers like arabino galactan pullulan and aid the internalization by clathrin-mediated endocytosis [59]. The enhanced signal was retained in the liver for more than 30min, before got eliminated. The clearance of the particles through kidney was later confirmed from the bio distribution studies, proving the absence of unnecessary deposition of the particles. Thus, the iron-based contrast agent with liver targeting ability, which can generate hyper intense signals on T1 weighted imaging and gets cleared from the body after imaging purpose is proposed as a potential probe for liver imaging.

Finally, to function as a bimodal nano hybrid probe, the optical imaging efficacy of P@ZVI-Cdts nanoparticle was tested by the intravenous administration of the same in mice. Since the developed material had excitation-dependent emission, different excitations of the nanoparticle were tried. The emission was around 500 and 640 nm when excited at a wavelength of 430 and 540 nm respectively. The images obtained at 540 nm excitation showed much better signal-to-background ratio than that at 430 nm. Even though the fluorescence emission was weaker at the longer wavelengths, the decrease in autofluorescence background and an increase in the tissue penetration provided an increase in the signal to noise ratio with bright fluorescence. After 2 h of administration of the nanoparticles, maximum signal was observed from the liver, the gall bladder region, kidney and urinary bladder (Fig. 7 a) during live animal imaging. This result again confirms the liver targeting property of P@ZVI-Cdts due to the presence of pullulan and its elimination through the bile when intravenously administrated. Fluorescence in the kidney and urinary bladder is attributed to the uptake of P@ZVI-Cdts by the glomerulus and the excretion pathway followed by the nanoparticles. Moreover, it has already been established that the presence of carbon dots will help to promote the renal clearances [60]. To know the biodistribution of the P@ZVI-Cdts in different organs ex vivo imaging was carried out by harvesting the organs after 4 h of intravenous injection. The results were in acquiescent with the in vivo imaging results showing maximum fluorescence intensity from the liver and gall bladder (Fig. 7b), confirming the liver targeting potential of the final hybrid system for specific optical bioimaging.

The main goal of this work was to develop a nontoxic ZVI based contrast agent for magnetic resonance angiography and a hybrid nanoprobe for targeted multimodal imaging. The novelty of the work relies on the establishment of C@ZVI and P@ZVI-Cdts as potential candidates as contrast agents in the medical diagnostics. To overcome the shortcomings of the currently used gadolinium complexes, like causing nephrogenic toxicity to renally impaired patients and the deposition of Gd on different organs including brain, we have attempted to develop a new iron based positive contrast agent for MRA. The citrate stabilized zerovalent iron core-shell structure was nontoxic and biocompatible with low magnetization when compared to that of super paramagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles. The small magnetic moment and spin canting effect contributed to the paramagnetic nature of ZVI which is suitable to shorten the spin-lattice relaxation of protons contributing to the T1 contrast for MRI and MRA. The citrate coated zerovalent iron nanoparticle exhibited r1 relaxivity value 4.93 mM−1s−1, which is better than that of the currently used gadolinium based systems. The low r2/r1 ratio of 6.03 recommends C@ZVI based system as an efficient T1 contrast agent. C@ZVI was further modified with liver targeting polysaccharide, pullulan and finally with fluorescent carbon dots for using it as dual imaging contrast agent. The efficiency of the developed systems for magnetic resonance angiography, targeted liver imaging and optical imaging has been successfully demonstrated in vivo. The non-toxicity, high r1 relaxivity and low synthesis cost enable zerovalent iron based nanoparticles to be a competent T1 MRI contrast agent for various clinical applications including diagnosis of the myocardial infarction, renal failure, atherosclerotic plaque, thrombosis, liver fibrosis and angiogenesis of tumor cells, in future. Also, by integrating the techniques of MRI and optical imaging, the study throws light on a potential system for multimodal imaging in future.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support received from Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology under the OHF project. Authors also thank the Scientists-In- Charge and staff of BST, Central Analytical Facility, BCL and DTERT divisions of BMT Wing and HOD, IS &IR for extending their support in using some of their facilities.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: N. Nimi, S. Ariya, S.N. Shaiju, F. Nimmi, J.S. Sachin, R.S. Jayasree, Biosafety of citrate coated zerovalent iron nanoparticles for magnetic resonance angiography, Data in brief, Biomaterials (in press).
